_meta.notes: Chapter 1 Lesson 5. Previously taught: kumusta, mabuti, ikaw, magandang, umaga, gabi, po, naman, sige.

title: Yes and No
subtitle: The Basics of Agreeing and Disagreeing

cards.body: Two of the most important words in any language. Let's learn how to say yes and no.
Oo means 'yes.' Short, simple, and you'll use it constantly.
Hindi means 'no.' Also used to negate things, like 'not' in English.

cards.source_text: Your friend asks: Do you want to come?
cards.prompt: You want to go. What do you say?

cards.body: Remember po? Add it to oo and it becomes opo. The respectful yes.

example.note: Oo + po = opo. The most respectful way to say yes.

cards.source_text: Your grandmother asks: Have you eaten?
cards.explanation: Opo = yes (respectful). Oo + po merged into one word.

cards.body: For a respectful no, just add po after hindi. They stay as two words.

example.note: Unlike oo/opo, hindi and po don't merge.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: Hindi the Tagalog word and Hindi the Indian language are completely unrelated. Just a coincidence.

cards.source_text: Your boss asks: Can you work this Saturday?
cards.prompt: You can't. How do you say no respectfully?
cards.explanation: Hindi po = no (respectful). Two separate words, unlike opo.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice.

alam_mo_ba: Filipinos sometimes say 'oo' multiple times for emphasis: 'Oo, oo, oo!' means a very enthusiastic yes.
Hindi is also used like 'not' in sentences. 'Hindi mabuti' = not good. You'll learn more about this later.
Some regions say 'oho' instead of 'opo.' Same meaning, just a regional flavour.
